narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, coming from a middle-income family and planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from past students' paths to the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to pass on the first try. For you, it estimated a 56.8% chance of failing the bar exam, which tipped the scales toward rejecting your application to help ensure we admit students with the highest predicted odds of success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance might not fully prepare you for the bar's demands. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also nudged things in that direction, though with less force, as it was similarly lower than average. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA pushed strongly against failure, aligning closely with the dataset average and showing real promise. Your LSAT score and middle-income background offered some helpful counterbalance too, suggesting potential strengths in test-taking and stability, while your full-time enrollment plan was a minor positive amid the mostly full-time applicant pool. Compared to typical students who pass, your decile rankings stand out as notably lower, which overshadowed these other solid aspects in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential—it's just how your profile stacked up against historical bar passage trends. To move forward, consider retaking courses or boosting your academic deciles through post-bac programs, prepping intensely for a higher LSAT, or gaining targeted experience like bar prep tutoring to strengthen your next application. We're rooting for you to turn this around and succeed. Keep pushing—you've got the drive.
